headline: Two losses to one-move mates — with 7 minutes on the clock
note: Three rapid games today, 1W-2L, and both losses ended the same way: a mate-in-1 you allowed while blitzing. Against s4rc4stic, 18.gxf3 just wins the checking knight — instead 18.Kh1 let Qxh2# land. Against Hamid137 you were a whole knight up after 14...Nxe7, but that knight on c6 was the only defender of your d8 rook: Qxd8# back-rank mate, on a move that took six seconds. Final clocks: 6:41, 7:46, 8:31 — in 10-minute games. The fix is not more chess knowledge, it is pace: a recapture is still a move, and every move gets the two-question scan (can I be mated in one? can I mate in one?). The win vs ArunKumar was a gift — you were down 4 points when they quit. Bright spot: repertoire 3-for-3 (London twice, Caro-Kann once) and castling on moves 8 and 9 in the games that mattered. Fuller picture: yesterday's 21-game marathon (8W-12L-1D) is analyzed in coach-log/2026-07-21-marathon.md — same story at scale: winning positions lost to the clock.

focus.title: Mate-in-1 scan — both ways
focus.detail: Both of today's losses were one-move mates you allowed with a full clock; retrain the every-move scan.

tasks.title: Mate-in-1 scan — both ways
tasks.detail: Both losses today ended on an allowed mate-in-1 played in seconds (18.Kh1 vs s4rc4stic, Qxd8# vs Hamid137). Twelve puzzles to re-arm the two-question scan.

tasks.title: Convert a won game — at speed
tasks.detail: Yesterday's marathon threw away +6, +7 and +8 positions to the clock. One conversion drill played fast: trade, simplify, move.

tasks.title: One King's Indian course lesson
tasks.detail: 0-for-4 vs 1.d4 in the marathon and it stays your highest weakness weight. Do the next unit in the KID course.
